perturbation.original_text: Vendor shall give CVS notice immediately if CVS Information has been accessed by unauthorized individuals or if there is any evidence of any such infraction.
perturbation.changed_text: Vendor shall give CVS notice within 30 days if CVS Information has been accessed by unauthorized individuals or if there is any evidence of any such infraction.
perturbation.explanation: Delaying the notification period to 30 days contradicts data breach notification laws that require immediate or very prompt notification to affected parties and regulators.  Delaying such notification could lead to more damage to CVS.
perturbation.contradicted_law: New York State Information Security Breach and Notification Act - General Business Law § 899-aa
perturbation.law_explanation: New York's data breach notification law, General Business Law § 899-aa, requires notification to affected individuals and the state attorney general 'in the most expedient time possible and without unreasonable delay' following the discovery of a breach.  A 30-day delay in notifying CVS directly violates this requirement for prompt notification.
perturbation.justification: The changed text modifies the notification timeframe from 'immediately' to 'within 30 days'. This directly contradicts the legal requirement for prompt notification following a data breach, as detailed in the Law Explanation referencing New York's General Business Law § 899-aa, which mandates notification 'in the most expedient time possible and without unreasonable delay'. Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing Article 39-F of the General Business Law, further supports the relevance of data security protections and notification requirements. The 30-day delay clearly undermines the requirement for immediate notification, establishing a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: the obligations o f each party to maintain the confidentiality of the I nformation it has received under this Agreement shall continue for the later to occur of (i) a peri od of three (3) years after such termination, or (i i) if this Agreement is incorporated by reference into another agreement between the parties, the term, including extensions, of that agreement, plus three (3) years.
perturbation.changed_text: the obligations o f each party to maintain the confidentiality of the I nformation it has received under this Agreement shall continue for the later to occur of (i) a peri od of one (1) year after such termination, or (i i) if this Agreement is incorporated by reference into another agreement between the parties, the term, including extensions, of that agreement, plus one (1) year.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the confidentiality period to one year may be insufficient to protect trade secrets under New York law, potentially undermining the reasonable measures required to maintain secrecy.
perturbation.contradicted_law: New York Uniform Trade Secrets Act (NYUTSA) - Article 24-A of the General Business Law
perturbation.law_citation: Article 24-A of the General Business Law
perturbation.law_explanation: Under the NYUTSA, trade secret protection lasts as long as the information remains a secret and provides a competitive advantage.  Reducing the confidentiality period to one year may be deemed insufficient to maintain secrecy, especially for information with a longer useful life, and could be viewed as a failure to take reasonable measures to protect the trade secret, thus jeopardizing its legal protection.
perturbation.justification: The change from a three-year to a one-year confidentiality period directly undermines the protection of trade secrets under NY law, as explained in both the Perturbation Explanation and the Law Explanation. The Law Explanation states that trade secret protection lasts as long as the information remains a secret and provides a competitive advantage and that reducing the confidentiality period to one year may be deemed insufficient to maintain secrecy. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 does not directly discuss trade secrets, it provides context that New York law covers various business-related regulations, implying that trade secret protection falls within its purview. The one-year period creates a potential contradiction if it is not considered a reasonable measure to protect the trade secret, thereby jeopardizing its legal protection.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed by and construed a nd enforced in accordance with the laws of the State of New York, without regard to its choice of law provisions.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be governed by and construed a nd enforced in accordance with the laws of the State of California, without regard to its choice of law provisions.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the governing law to California when the contract explicitly involves a Rhode Island corporation and a vendor with potentially no connection to California introduces a conflict.  California law may not be appropriate.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Choice of Law Principles - Restatement (Second) of Conflict of Laws § 187
perturbation.law_explanation: The Restatement (Second) of Conflict of Laws § 187 states that the law chosen by the parties to govern their contractual rights and duties will be applied if the particular issue is one which the parties could have resolved by an explicit provision in their agreement directed to that issue. However, the law will not be applied if it has no substantial relationship to the parties or the transaction and there is no other reasonable basis for the parties' choice or the application of the chosen law would be contrary to a fundamental policy of a state which has a materially greater interest than the chosen state in the determination of the particular issue. Since CVS is from Rhode Island, and the Vendor's location is blank, moving the choice of law to California would violate this law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Penn WebLogin Log in with your PennKey Username Password Log in Forgot username? Forgot password? Change my password Test my PennKey Privacy policy
perturbation.justification: The original text specifies New York law, while the changed text specifies California law. The Perturbation Explanation points out that the changed text introduces a conflict because the contract involves a Rhode Island corporation and a vendor with potentially no connection to California, making California law potentially inappropriate. The Law Explanation references the Restatement (Second) of Conflict of Laws § 187, stating that the chosen law should have a substantial relationship to the parties or transaction. Because CVS is from Rhode Island and the Vendor's location is blank, moving the choice of law to California may violate this law. This change in governing law undermines the principle that the chosen law should bear a reasonable relationship to the parties or the transaction, thus creating a legal contradiction. The absence of relevant scraped law snippets does not change the fact that the modified text contradicts the law explanation provided. The change is an inconsistency that creates a legal contradiction as specified in the definitions.

perturbation.original_text: Within ten (10) days following the receipt of a written request referencing this Agreement and this paragraph from either party furnishing Information hereunder, the receiving party will del iver to the furnishing party all tangible materials containing or embodying the Information received fr om the furnishing party, except for materials containing Information which has been incorporated into analyses, compilations, comparisons, studies or other documents prepared by the receivin g party or its representatives, together with a certificate executed by the receiving party certify ing that all such materials in the receiving party' s
perturbation.changed_text: Within ten (10) days following the receipt of an oral request referencing this Agreement and this paragraph from either party furnishing Information hereunder, the receiving party will del iver to the furnishing party all tangible materials containing or embodying the Information received fr om the furnishing party, except for materials containing Information which has been incorporated into analyses, compilations, comparisons, studies or other documents prepared by the receivin g party or its representatives, together with a certificate executed by the receiving party certify ing that all such materials in the receiving party' s
perturbation.explanation: Changing the requirement for a 'written request' to an 'oral request' can create ambiguity and enforceability issues, especially for compliance with legal and regulatory requirements. This could create legal uncertainty.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Statute of Frauds - New York General Obligations Law § 5-701
perturbation.law_explanation: New York's Statute of Frauds (General Obligations Law § 5-701) requires certain agreements to be in writing to be enforceable. While the specific requirement to return confidential information might not always fall under the Statute of Frauds, removing the 'written request' requirement opens the door to disputes about whether a request was actually made and its terms, creating enforceability problems that could conflict with the need for clear, documented agreements, especially if trade secrets or sensitive data are involved. A written request provides a clear record and avoids ambiguity.

See most recent version before or on: 2014-09-22 2015-10-09 2017-10-27 2018-04-20 2018-07-06 2018-07-13 2018-08-31 2019-01-11 2019-01-18 2019-06-28 2019-07-05 2019-07-19 2019-08-16 2019-10-18 2019-11-22 2019-12-13 2020-02-21 2020-04-10 2020-12-18 2021-01-08 2021-04-02 2021-04-09 2021-06-18 2021-09-24 2021-12-31 2022-02-25 2022-03-04 2022-12-30 2023-01-06 2023-10-27 2023-11-26 2024-12-20 2025-01-03 Share Facebook Email SECTION 5-701 Agreements required to be in writing General Obligations (GOB) CHAPTER 24-A, ARTICLE 5, TITLE 7 § 5-701. Agreements required to be in writing. a. Every agreement, promise or undertaking is void, unless it or some note or memorandum thereof be in writing, and subscribed by the party to be charged therewith, or by his lawful agent, if such agreement, promise or undertaking: 1. By its terms is not to be performed within one year from the making thereof or the performance of which is not to be completed before the end of a lifetime; 2. Is a special promise to answer for the debt, default or miscarriage of another person; 3. Is made in consideration of marriage, except mutual promises to marry; 5. Is a subsequent or new promise to pay a debt discharged in bankruptcy; 6. Notwithstanding section 2-201 of the uniform commercial code, if the goods be sold at public auction, and the auctioneer at the time of the sale, enters in a sale book, a memorandum specifying the nature and price of the property sold, the terms of the sale, the name of the purchaser, and the name of the person on whose account the sale was made, such memorandum is equivalent in effect to a note of the contract or sale, subscribed by the party to be charged therewith; 9. Is a contract to assign or an assignment, with or without consideration to the promisor, of a life or health or accident insurance policy, or a promise, with or without consideration to the promisor, to name a beneficiary of any such policy. This provision shall not apply to a policy of industrial life or health or accident insurance. 10. Is a contract to pay compensation for services rendered in negotiating a loan, or in negotiating the purchase, sale, exchange, renting or leasing of any real estate or interest therein, or of a business opportunity, business, its good will, inventory, fixtures or an interest therein, including a majority of the voting stock interest in a corporation and including the creating of a partnership interest.

perturbation.justification: The change from 'written request' to 'oral request' directly undermines the enforceability and clarity of the agreement, potentially conflicting with New York's Statute of Frauds, as explained in the Law Explanation. While the statute primarily addresses agreements that cannot be performed within one year, the principle of requiring written documentation for significant contractual obligations applies here. Scraped Law Snippets 1 and 2 both reference NY GOB § 5-701, which states that certain agreements are void unless in writing. Though the return of tangible materials isn't explicitly listed, the lack of written record for the request introduces ambiguity and enforceability issues, creating a legal contradiction as it weakens the ability to prove a request was made and its terms.
